coredata.dc:description: The accumulation of bilirubin in blood is associated with many diseases. Sensitive and accurate detection of bilirubin is of great significance for personal health care. The rapid development of fluorescent nanomaterials promotes rapid development in the bilirubin assay. In this review, traditional methods for detection of bilirubin are briefly presented to compare with fluorescent nanosensors. Subsequently, the recent progress of different types of fluorescent nanomaterials for determination of bilirubin is summarized. Further, the performance of fluorescent nanosensors and conventional techniques for sensing bilirubin are compared. To this end, the challenges and prospects concerning the topics are discussed. This review will provide some introductory knowledge for researchers to understand the status and importance of fluorescent nanosensors for sensing bilirubin.

head.ce:title: Nanomaterials for fluorescent assay of bilirubin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accumulation of bilirubin in blood is associated with many diseases. Sensitive and accurate detection of bilirubin is of great significance for personal health care. The rapid development of fluorescent nanomaterials promotes rapid development in the bilirubin assay. In this review, traditional methods for detection of bilirubin are briefly presented to compare with fluorescent nanosensors. Subsequently, the recent progress of different types of fluorescent nanomaterials for determination of bilirubin is summarized. Further, the performance of fluorescent nanosensors and conventional techniques for sensing bilirubin are compared. To this end, the challenges and prospects concerning the topics are discussed. This review will provide some introductory knowledge for researchers to understand the status and importance of fluorescent nanosensors for sensing bilirubin.

In this review, recent developments about various fluorescent nanomaterials for fluorescent detection of bilirubin are presented.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Bilirubin (BR), a metabolite of heme, is excreted via bile. The normal level of BR serves as a strong antioxidant to protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, such as coronary artery disease, atherosclerosis and diabetic complications [1,2]. However, the accumulation of BR in blood can cause hyperbilirubinemia. The elevated level of BR accumulation is associated with the symptoms of yellow coloration of skin and sclera in association with jaundice [3]. The high level of BR can result in neurotoxicity in newborns, such as hearing loss, athetosis, brain damage and so on [3]. Jaundice in adults can also lead to inflamed liver or obstructed bile duct [4]. The normal concentration of serum BR is found to be less than 25 μM in a healthy human, but for the person in jaundice conditions, the BR level is greater than 50 μM. The BR concentration is above 170 μM and 340 μM for adults and newborns in hyperbilirubinemiaimic conditions, respectively [5]. Therefore, a sensitive and rapid assay of BR is of considerable significance for early diagnosis and treatment of hyperbilirubinemiaemia.

Up to now, a variety of approaches have been well developed for determination of BR, such as liquid chromatography [6,7], capillary electrophoresis [8], Raman spectroscopy [9], electrochemistry [10], colorimetry [11], fluorescence [12–16], etc. These instrumental approaches exhibited excellent sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy for the determination of BR, but these methods requires expensive instruments, time-consuming sample pre-treatment, complicated operation procedures and professional operators, making them not easily affordable in some resource-limited regions. The electrochemical method needs the involvement of enzyme and fabrication of electrodes, and the activity of enzyme is easily affected by the environment. The stability of electrode also affects the detection results. The colorimetric method is simple for BR assay, but the sensitivity needs to be improved. The traditional molecular fluorescent approach is a good candidate for sensing BR due to its simplicity and high sensitivity. Nonetheless, the synthesis of fluorescent probes involved in complex organic synthetic procedures, plenty of organic solvents and bad water solubility. Particularly the nanomaterials-based fluorescent methods exhibited unique merits, such as ease of fabrication, high sensitivity and affordability.

In the past few years, various nanomaterials, such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), Eu(III)-doped yttrium oxide nanosheets, MoS2 quantum dots (MoS2 QDs), fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (poly-NPs), fluorescent metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), fluorescent metal nanoclusters (NCs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have been demonstrated for the fluorescent detection of BR (Fig. 1 ). However, there was no systematic review about this topic. Although there are some reviews to summarize the BR detection methods [17–19], these reviews rarely refer to the nanomaterials-based fluorescent sensors. In the light of considerable achievement of nanotechnology, it is crucial to highlight the advances of nanomaterials-based fluorescent probes for BR. In this review, the conventional techniques for detection of BR are briefly provided. The advances of various fluorescent nanomaterials for the fluorescent detection of BR were further summarized, including semiconductor QDs, Eu(III)-doped yttrium oxide nanosheets, MoS2 QDs, fluorescent poly-NPs, fluorescent MOFs, fluorescent metal NCs and CQDs. The comparison of the performance between traditional methods and fluorescent nanosensors for sensing BR is also discussed. Lastly, the challenges and perspectives about this topic were provided.

Up to now, various conventional methods have been developed for detection of BR, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), electrochemistry, fluorescence and so on. In this section, we will briefly present these conventional methods for sensing BR.

HPLC is a standard and sensitive method for determination of BR. Franko's group proposed a HPLC combined with ultra-sensitive thermal lens spectrometric (TLS) method for simultaneous detection of biliverdin and bilirubin in human serum. Isocratic reversed-phase C18 HPLC and TLS based on excitation by a krypton laser emission line at 407 nm were used in the approach. IX-α biliverdin and IX-α bilirubin were successfully separated within 11 min. The limit of detection (LOD) for biliverdin and BR were 1.2 nM and 1 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the step-gradient elution could enhance the LOD for BR to be 0.5 nM. The ratio of free bilirubin versus biliverdin in human serum was also determined to be around 3:1 by the method [20]. The HPLC combined with TLS provides a sensitive approach for monitoring BR and biliverdin in human serum.

SERS is a sensitive detection technique for sensing BR. Based on the in vivo chemical interaction between glucuronic acid and BR, Ouyang et al. have construct a SERS for biomimetic point-of-care testing BR using glucose functionalized Au NPs. The method was further fabricated into a portable point-of-care testing devices by using glucose-Au array chip, a handhold Raman spectrometer and internal standard calibration method. As a result, sensitive assay of free BR was realized with a LOD of 0.1 nM. Moreover, the method was successfully utilized for determination of the binding constant between BR and human serum albumin (HSA) [21]. Gold-decorated silicon nanowires [22] and graphene oxide-plasmonic gold nanostar hybrids decorated on the filter paper [23] were prepared as SERS-active substrate for sensitive assay of free BR.

As an electrically active substance, BR can also be assayed by electrochemical methods. For example, a molecularly imprinting (MIP) electrochemical method has been proposed for sensing BR in saliva. The glassy carbon electrode was first modified by multi-walled carbon nanotubes. O-phenylenediamine was further electropolymerized in the presence of BR to fabricate MIP electrode. After release of BR and washing, the electrode was used for amperometric detection of BR with a linearity of 12.08–91.81 fM and a LOD of 7.80 fM [24]. Also, a bioelectrode was prepared for amperometric assay of BR. The bioelectrode was fabricated via the electrodeposition of graphene oxide NPs decorated nickel NPs layer and further modified with BR oxidase. This method exhibited a rapid electrocatalytic response towards BR with a linear range of 0.01–600 μM. Furthermore, this sensor exhibited good stability. The sensing performance was also comparable to that of the standard colorimetric technique [25].

Fluorescence method was also applied for sensing BR. For instance, 2,2’-(1E,1′E)-((6-bromopyridine-2,3-diyl) bis(azanylylidene)) bis(-methanylylidene diphenol was synthesized for fluorescence light-off detection of BR in human biofluids through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). The fabricated probe exhibited high quantum yield and sensitive response to BR with a wide linear scope of 1 pM–500 μM [26]. Recently, a benzorhodol-derivative-attached N-oxide probe has been prepared for fluorescence “turn-on” determination of BR in the presence of Fe3 + based on a deoxygenation switching mechanism. Upon the addition of BR, Fe3 + was transformed into Fe2+, which in turn induces a deoxygenation reaction with the probe to produce benzorhodol derivative with bright red emission. As a result, BR was successfully qualified with a LOD of 33 nM [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials for fluorescent assay of BR
ce:section.ce:para: In the recent years, a variety of nanomaterials have been utilized for fluorescent assay of BR. For these nanomaterials-based fluorescent sensors for BR, there are mainly three fluorescence response modes: fluorescence “turn-off”, fluorescence “turn-on” and ratiometric fluorescence. In the fluorescence “turn-off” sensors, BR can quench the fluorescence of nanomaterials via inner filter effect (IFE), FRET and photo-induced electron transfer (PET). For IFE, BR shows strong absorption between 400 and 500 nm, which would overlap with the excitation and/or emission spectrum of the probes, resulting in fluorescence quenching [28]. In FRET and PET, BR specifically interacts with the probes to form a complex, which accelerates FRET and PET, decreasing the fluorescence [29–31]. The decreased fluorescence signal is associated with the concentration of BR, which is utilized for detection of BR (Fig. 2 A). For the fluorescence “turn-on” probes, quenching agents first repress the fluorescence of the sensor, and the addition of BR can recover the fluorescence of the sensing system [32,33]. The enhancement of fluorescence intensity correlates with the concentration of BR (Fig. 2B). Regarding the ratiometric fluorescent sensors, dual-emission is demanded. BR prefers to repress one emission, and the other fluorescence emission behavior remains unchanged [34,35]. The ratio of two fluorescence intensities were used to quantify BR (Fig. 2C). In this section, we will highlight the recent progress of various nanomaterials for fluorescent determination of BR.

Semiconductor QDs have been developed a variety of fluorescent nanosensors due to their extraordinary optical properties [36]. Undoubtedly, semiconductor QDs are also used for the fluorescent detection of BR. For example, bovine serum albumin (BSA)-stabilized CdSe QDs (BSA-CdSe QDs) were prepared for fluorescence “light-off” determination of BR because of the strong specific interactions between BSA and BR. This strong interaction promoted electrons transfer from the excited QDs to BR, causing the fluorescence quenching of BSA-CdSe QDs. As a result, submicromolar level of BR was successfully assayed, and the possible interfering coexistent substances showed no obvious effect on the assay of BR. Importantly, the developed approach was capable of sensing free BR even at least two orders of magnitude lower than BR level in normal blood [37]. Similarly, l-cysteine modified Mn:ZnS QDs prepared from a microwave-assisted wet chemical method were also utilized for fluorescence “turn-off” sensing BR due to the reductive PET caused by the interaction of BR with manganese and zinc ions of excited QDs (Fig. 3 A). BR was selectively recognized with a linearity of 10.99–63.84 μM and a LOD of 1.8 μM. Meanwhile, some co-existing biomolecules displayed negligible influence on the assay of BR. Furthermore, the practical applications of the detection system were evaluated in spiked human serum and urine samples with good recovery. Importantly, a paper-strip based sensor was also constructed, revealing the promising applications of the sensor [30]. These semiconductor QDs-based sensors showed satisfactory performance for sensing BR, yet the toxicity of heavy metal cannot be ignored.

Compared with Eu(III) complexes, Eu(III)-doped yttrium oxide nanosheets possessed two-dimensional anisotropy, enhanced luminescence and higher stability, exhibiting potential applications in bio/chemical sensing. Recently, polystyrene-encapsulated 2-thenoyltrifluoroacetonate tailored Eu(III)-doped yttrium oxide nanosheets have been fabricated into fluorescent films through electrophoretic deposition and spin coating. The fabricated sensing films exhibited the maximum at 614 nm upon excitation of 356 nm, which was attributed to the 5D0-7F2 transition of Eu(III). Surprisingly, free BR could selectively quench the fluorescence of the sensing films via FRET. Consequently, the fluorescence “turn-off” detection system displayed good sensitivity towards free BR over the linear range of 0.0–60.0 μM and a LOD of 41 nM [29]. Disappointingly, the practicability of the sensing system was not accessed.

As a novel type of fluorescent nanomaterials, MoS2 QDs have attracted considerable attention due to their good stability, unique fluorescence feature and excellent biocompatibility [38]. Fluorescence assay is one of their most important applications. Similarly, the blue-emissive MoS2 QDs from hydrothermal treatment of sodium molybdate and cysteine were used for fluorescence “turn-off” detection of BR via FRET and IFE (Fig. 3B). As a result, sensitive assay of BR was accomplished with a linear range of 0.5–10 μM and a LOD of 2.1 nM. Particularly, some common biomolecules showed little impact on the assay of BR. More importantly, the developed sensor was successfully applied in human serum and urine samples [28]. However, the present MoS2 QDs-based nanosensors suffer from low quantum yield and short wavelength emission. Bright MoS2 QDs with tunable emission are, thus, highly desirable.

Fluorescent poly-NPs have been widely demonstrated in bioimaging, diagnosis, therapy and biochemical sensing due to their small size, tunable emission behavior, easy modification and high brightness [39–42]. Conjugated fluorescent poly-NPs have been prepared from poly 1,1′-((2,7-dimethyl-9H-fluorene-9,9-diyl)bis(hexane-6,1-diyl))bis(1H-benzo[d]imidazole) for fluorescent detection of BR in aqueous solution. The poly-NPs showed yellow fluorescence, which was suppressed by BR due to FRET caused by the ground state complexation between BR and the poly-NPs (Fig. 4 A). The receptor benzimidazole exhibited specific interaction with BR, greatly enhancing the selectivity and sensitivity of the sensing system. Consequently, the LOD was as low as 6.9 pM, which was far below the clinical assay range of 25–50 μM. More importantly, BR in blood serum samples was successfully quantified, which was in good agreement with the standard peroxidase method. Interestingly, a paper-based sensor was fabricated, providing a rapid and reliable assay platform for BR [43]. Also, the tetraphenylethylene modified hyperbranched polyamidoamine and rhodamine B formed fluorescent poly-NPs were established as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for sensing BR. The formed sensing system displayed dual emission at 465 nm and 577 nm. The fluorescence at 465 nm arising from the poly-NPs was effectively quenched by FRET between the poly-NPs and BR, yet the peak at 577 nm from rhodamine B was not sensitive to BR, resulting in a fluorescence color change from blue to orange. The fluorescence intensity ratios at 465 nm and 577 nm were utilized for the determination of BR, producing a linear range of 50 nM-8.5 μM and a LOD of 25 nM. The common biomolecules and anions exhibited no significant effect on the assay of BR, demonstrating the good selectivity of the sensing system. Furthermore, the practicability of the detection system was conducted in urine samples with good recovery. The sensing system was fabricated into a portable hydrogel-based point-of-care testing device for BR, providing the convenience for on-site monitoring [34]. Similarly, oligo(p-phenylenevinylene) incorporated in the amphiphilic glucuronic acid modified polystyrene nanobeads have been fabricated for fluorescence “switch-off” detection of BR due to energy transfer caused by noncovalent interaction. The LOD for BR assay was as low as 20 nM [44]. Although many advantages are found in the poly-NPs-based fluorescent nanosensors, the fluorescent poly-NPs require complicated organic synthesis and tedious operations.

Recently, lanthanide-based MOFs have emerged as powerful candidates for constructing a variety of fluorescent sensors due to their unique emission, long fluorescence lifetime and large Stokes shift [45,46]. Highly stable Eu(III)-based MOFs with bent Eu(COO) chains interconnected by pairs of T-shaped 4,4’-(4,4′-bipyridine-2,6-diyl)dibenzoate linkers have been solvothermally prepared and designed a fluorescent probe for rapid recognition of BR. The as-prepared MOFs exhibited red emission, which was significantly suppressed by BR because of IFE and PET, generating a fluorescence “turn-off” sensor for BR with a LOD of 1.75 μM. Excitingly, the senor could be further constructed a portable test paper, providing a convenient technique for visual accessing BR levels in clinical applications [47].

The post-synthetic functionalization of small molecules can generate fluorescent MOFs. UIO-66-NH2 was modified by 2,3,4-trihydroxybenzaldehyde via aldimine condensation reaction to form fluorescent MOFs. The fluorescence of the as-prepared fluorescent MOFs could be quenched by BR via FRET (Fig. 4B), resulting in a fluorescence “turn-off” sensor for rapid identification of BR with a LOD of 0.59 pM. The feasibility of the sensing system was further demonstrated in human serum samples [48]. In the light of the same strategy, 2,3,4-trihydroxy benzaldehyde modified Al(III) based MIL-53 MOFs were also prepared a fluorescence “turn-off” probe for BR with a LOD of 1.26 pM [49].

The fluorescence of MOFs can be also endowed via post-synthetic modification of lanthanide. For example, Eu(III) post-functionalized Zr(IV)-based MOFs (Zr-MOFs:Eu3+) were synthesized and utilized for fluorescence “turn-off” sensing BR due to the FRET between BR and the ligands. The devised sensor showed a rapid response, good selectivity and high sensitivity, and the linear range and LOD was 0–15 μM and 0.45 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the practicability of the sensing system was confirmed in the human serum samples. Interestingly, the Zr-MOFs:Eu3+ was fabricated into test paper for rapid visual assay of BR in real samples [31]. Based on the same strategy, Eu3+ and Tb3+ modified Cd(II)-based MOFs [50] and Tb3+@MOFs-808 [51] were also developed a fluorescence “turn-off” sensor for identification of BR. These MOFs-based fluorescent probes exhibited good performance for sensing BR, yet these probes suffered from the use of metal ions or complex ligands.

As novel fluorescent nanomaterials, a variety of fluorescent metal NCs have been constructed for fluorescence sensing, bioimaging, biomedicine, and other fields [52–55]. Similarly, a variety of fluorescent metal NCs including fluorescent gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) [32,56–60], fluorescent AgAu alloy nanoclusters (AgAuNCs) [61], fluorescent gold and molybdenum bimetallic nanoclusters (AuMoNCs) [62] and fluorescent copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) [63–67] have been demonstrated for fluorescent detection of BR. Owing to the strong binding affinity between HSA and BR, HSA-AuNCs were established for selective fluorescence “turn-off” assay of BR accordingly to the static quenching process. Accordingly, BR was successfully monitored with a linearity of 1–50 μM and a LOD of 248 nM. Moreover, the HSA-AuNCs exhibited peroxidase-like activity, which was also utilized for colorimetric determination of BR in the presence of H2O2. The developed sensor was conducted in human serum samples to verify the feasibility [56]. Similarly, BSA can also specifically interact with BR to form a complex, which can accelerate FRET between BR and the BSA-stabilized metal NCs, quenching the fluorescence of the fluorescent metal NCs. The blue and red emissive BSA-AuNCs film was prepared for ratiometric fluorescence recognition of BR via the layer-by-layer assembly technique. The film exhibited blue (443 nm) and red (622 nm) fluorescence upon excitation at 372 nm. The presence of BR could repress the two fluorescence signals at different degrees. Based on the change of the two fluorescence intensities, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for BR was successfully constructed, producing a linear relationship in the scope of 0.01–2.00 μM and a LOD of 8.9 nM. The feasibility was further evaluated in human blood serum samples [57]. In light of the same mechanism, the BSA-AuNCs could also be fabricated into a fluorescent film for fluorescence “switch-off” recognition of BR by using polydopamine film as adhesion layer. As a result, rapid determination of BR was accomplished with a linear scope from 0.8 μM to 50 μM and a LOD of 0.61 μM. Particularly, the fluorescent film was recyclable, and the common substances in human serum showed no interference with the assay of BR [58].

To enhance the binding sites for BR, glucuronic acid was used to modify HSA-AuNCs. The resulting glucuronic acid-modified HSA-AuNCs were further fabricated into an ultrathin film via the Langmuir-Blodgett (LB) technique. The addition of BR would quench the fluorescence of the film due to the specific interaction between BR and the glucuronic acid-modified HSA-AuNCs, yielding a fluorescence “turn-off” sensor for sensing BR with a linear relationship ranging from 1.00 nM to 5.00 μM and a LOD of 0.27 nM. Also, the sensor was successfully applied in human serum samples with good recoveries [59].

Similarly, the chitosan-stabilized Au NCs with yellow emission could be embedded in a polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane to produce a fluorescent film for fluorescent assay of BR in the presence of Cu2+. It was found that Cu2+ could quench the fluorescence of the film, while the addition of BR could restore the fluorescence due to the formation of Cu2+-BR complex. Consequently, fluorescence “switch-on” detection of BR was achieved with a sensitivity of 6.4 × 10−5 mg/mL. This film sensor made the detection of BR to be possible through thumb impression as well as using blood serum of the patient. Interestingly, the sensitivity of detection of BR could be turned by the concentration of Cu2+ [32].

Furthermore, GSH-stabilized AuNCs with aggregation-induced emission could be encapsulated in MOFs (ZIF-8) to construct a sensitive fluorescent probe (AuNCs@ZIF-8) for fluorescence “turn-off” sensing BR. The confinement effect considerably enhanced the quantum yield of AuNCs@ZIF-8 and fluorescence lifetime. Surprisingly, the addition of BR could destroy the skeleton of AuNCs@ZIF-8 due to the combination of BR and Zn2+ of ZIF, greatly decreasing the fluorescence at 590 nm of AuNCs@ZIF-8. Based on the fluorescence quenching effect, AuNCs@ZIF-8 was successfully applied for determination of BR with a linear scope of 0.1–5.0 μM and a LOD of 0.07 μM (Fig. 5 A). The AuNCs@ZIF-8 displayed good selectivity towards BR, and was also utilized for determination of BR in human serum [60].

Fluorescent alloy and bimetallic NCs were also utilized for fluorescent assay of BR. The red emissive AgAu NCs stabilized by wheat gluten have been fabricated through a galvanic exchange reaction. The AgAu NCs exhibited great stability and were employed for fluorescence “turn-off” assay of BR through PET and IFE, yielding linear ranges of 0.5–10 μM and 10–70 μM, and a LOD of 50 nM. The probe displayed good selectivity over other biomolecules. Importantly, the sensor was successfully applied in blood samples [61]. Lysozyme-templated fluorescent AuMo NCs with red emission have been prepared for the selective fluorescence “turn-off” assay of BR via FRET and IFE. As a consequence, BR was sensitively identified by the AuMo NCs with a linearity ranging from 0.125 μM to 40.00 μM and a LOD of 28.26 nM. Furthermore, the probe was validated by applying it for the quantification of BR in spiked plasma and urine samples of jaundice patients [62].

Compared with the gold-based fluorescent NCs, fluorescent copper NCs (CuNCs) possess low cost, ease of fabrication, tunable emission behavior and so on [55,63]. Therefore, fluorescent CuNCs have been widely applied in sensing, bioimaging, biomedicine and other related fields [55,63,68,69]. l-cysteine-stabilized fluorescent CuNCs (L-cys-CuNCs) were also employed for fluorescence “turn off” detection of BR based on the static quenching process. As a result, BR was successfully determined by L-cys-CuNCs with a narrow linear range of 1–10 μM and a LOD of 0.229 μM. Meanwhile, L-cys-CuNCs were also demonstrated for fluorescence “turn-off” assay of biliverdin. The practical utility of the sensor was demonstrated in artificial blood serum and urine samples [64].

Glutathione tailored copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) were constructed a dual-channel optical sensor for BR and biliverdin. It was observed that BR and biliverdin specifically interact with GSH-CuNCs to form a non-fluorescent ground state complex, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of GSH-CuNCs and color change. By virtue of the fluorescence quenching phenomenon, BR and biliverdin were successfully monitored. As a result, a linear scope of 0.8–9 μM and a LOD of 0.148 μM for BR assay were fulfilled. The obvious color change was also employed for colorimetric detection of BR and biliverdin. Moreover, the common biomolecules displayed little effect on the assay of BR. The feasibility of the sensing system was conducted in serum and urine samples [65].

Similarly, BR exhibited strong interaction with HSA-stabilized fluorescent CuNCs (HSA-CuNCs) to yield the non-fluorescent complex, greatly reducing the red fluorescence of the HSA-CuNCs. Thus, fluorescence “turn-off” assay of BR was successfully accomplished with two linear ranges of 1.25–7.5 μM and 5–28.75 μM. The LOD was 35 nM and 145 nM (Fig. 5B). The practicability of the sensor was demonstrated in human blood serum and urine samples [66]. Also, BSA could interact with BR to yield a complex. The BSA-CuNCs showed good selectivity and sensitivity, and were utilized for monitoring BR with a good linearity ranging from 10 pM to 150 pM and a LOD of 257 fM. The feasibility of the sensor was evaluated in human urine. Moreover, the color change of the sensor was also used to construct a colorimetric probe for BR, which was further fabricated into paper strip sensor for on-site monitoring BR [70].

In the light of antioxidant property of BR, the BSA-CuNCs/Fe3+ system was employed for fluorescence “turn-on” detection of BR. It was observed that Fe3+ could specifically quench the fluorescence of BSA-CuNCs through static quenching process. While the addition of BR to the BSA-CuNCs/Fe3+ system, the fluorescence of the sensing system recovered, this was attributed to the redox reactions between Fe3+ and BR. The constructed sensing system exhibited a good sensitivity towards BR with a linear relationship in the scope of 10–60 nM and a LOD of 6.62 nM. Furthermore, the feasibility of the sensor was investigated in urine and serum samples, and satisfactory results were achieved [67].

As a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, CQDs have been demonstrated in sensing, energy, environment, biomedicine and other related fields due to low-cost, ease of fabrication, low toxicity, high quantum yield, excellent photostability and other merits [71–74]. Nitrogen-doped CQDs with blue emission prepared from hydrothermal treatment of sucrose and l-proline exhibited quantum yield of 23.4% and good stability, and was utilized for fluorescence “turn-off” determination of BR due to the strong interaction between the CQDs and BR. Accordingly, BR was successfully assayed with a linear range of 0.35–9.78 μM and a LOD of 33 nM. The feasibility of the sensor was evaluated in human urine and blood serum samples [75]. Similarly, the blue emissive CQDs from sunflower seeds were employed for fluorescence “switch-off” detection of BR with a LOD of 2.03 μM [76].

The dynamic interaction between BR and CQDs can cause FRET, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the CQDs. By virtue of the mechanism, graphene QDs synthesized from refluxing graphene oxide in DMF solvent were utilized for fluorescence “turn-off” sensing BR. A wide linear range of 40.53–237.55 μM and a LOD of 9 μM were obtained. However, the real applications of the sensor were not demonstrated [77]. Based on a similar mechanism, the blue emitting nitrogen-doped CQDs derived from aspartic acid and 3,6-diaminoacridine hydrochloride were used for fluorescence “turn-off” determination of BR due to the formation of H-bonding network between BR and CQDs, producing a linearity range of 0–45 μM and a LOD of 89.07 nM. The practicability of the sensor was verified in human serum and urine samples [78].

The yellow emissive CQDs from the solvothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine were also used for fluorescence “turn-off” detection of BR due to the IFE. The CQDs exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity towards BR with a linear scope of 0.26–80 μM and a LOD of 88 nM. With the assistance of smartphone and 365 nm UV light, detection of BR was still achieved through computer software and RGB (red/green/blue) analysis. The linearity ranging from 4.0 μM to 225 μM and a LOD of 1.37 μM were obtained. Furthermore, the two sensing strategies based on the CQDs were successfully applied in newborns serum, adult serum and adult urine samples [79].

Sulfur and nitrogen doped CQDs were designed a fluorescence “turn-on” sensor for BR in the presence of Fe3+. The blue fluorescence of the CQDs was easily quenched by Fe3+ due to the static quenching process. Yet the addition of BR could restore the fluorescence, which was attributed to the strong binding affinity between BR and Fe3+. As a result, sensitive assay of BR was achieved with a linear scope of 0.2–2 nM and a LOD of 0.12 nM (Fig. 6 A). The sensor was successfully applied in spiked human serum and urine samples. Interestingly, the probe was further fabricated into a paper-based test stripe for visual assay of BR [33]. In view of the similar strategy, the green emissive CQDs from resorcinol and sucrose and Cu2+ were also designed a fluorescence “turn-on” probe for BR with a LOD of 85 nM [80].

Dual-emission CQDs from hydrogen peroxide oxidation polymerization of dopamine and o-phenylenediamine were utilized for ratiometric fluorescence assay of BR. The as-prepared CQDs showed two emissions centered at 460 nm and 540 nm upon the excitation of 400 nm. The presence of BR could selectively quench the fluorescence at 460 nm in an alkaline medium, and a new fluorescence emission peak at 560 gradually emerged (Fig. 6B). By virtue of the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 560 nm and 460 nm, the determination of BR was accomplished with a linear relationship ranging from 0 to 400 μM and a LOD of 7.24 μM. The feasibility was accessed in human saliva and fetal bovine serum samples [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of fluorescent nanosensors versus conventional methods
ce:section.ce:para: The conventional methods exhibited excellent performance for determination of BR, such as high sensitivity and selectivity. However, there are still some limitations in these traditional methods. HPLC displayed excellent selectivity. SERS and electrochemistry possess high sensitivity and rapid response. Yet, HPLC and SERS are expensive, and require complicated operations, which are not affordable in remote and developing regions. For electrochemical methods, the stability and repeatability of the electrodes demand further improvement. The fluorescent organic probes showed simplicity and good sensitivity towards BR assay, but complicated organic synthesis and plenty of organic solvents are demanded, and the water solubility of the probes is not good.

The fluorescent nanomaterials provide a facile approach for fluorescent detection of BR (Table 1 ). The synthesis of these fluorescent nanomaterials does not require expensive and complicated instruments, which can be easily conducted in most laboratories. Meanwhile, the operations are labor-saving, and most students can perform them. Though the performance is not comparable to some conventional methods, the sensitivity of almost all fluorescent nanosensors is at micromole levels, which are feasible for practical applications. Moreover, the response of most nanosensors towards BR is rapid. Therefore, the performance of fluorescent nanosensors for sensing BR is comparable to the traditional methods in some aspects. The development of fluorescent nanosensors for sensitive and selective determination of BR is still important, especially for the developing regions that can not be affordable to expensive instruments.

BR is derived from the catabolism of heme, and the accumulation of BR in blood can cause many diseases. Some traditional methods, such as HPLC, SERS, electrochemistry and fluorescence, displayed excellent sensitivity and good selectivity towards BR. However, these conventional approaches are limited by high-cost and complicated operations. The emergence of fluorescent nanomaterials facilitates the rapid development of nanosensors for sensing BR.

In this contribution, traditional methods for determination of BR have been briefly presented. Subsequently, the advances in the fluorescent nanomaterials for sensing BR have been highlighted in detail based on the fluorescence response modes. Further, the comparison of the performance of fluorescent nanosensors and conventional methods has been briefly discussed. These fluorescent nanosensors exhibited significant improvements in cost and speed. The sensitivity is also comparable to that of some traditional approaches. However, there are still some limitations in these fluorescent nanosensors. First, the sensitivity and selectivity should be further improved. The sensitivity of fluorescent nanosensors is at micromolar levels, and there is still some space to improve the sensitivity to nanomolar or femtomolar levels (Table 1). The sensitivity can be improved by enhancing the quantum yield of fluorescent nanomaterials or choosing novel assay strategies. Meanwhile, novel detection strategies may also improve the selectivity. Second, further studies are demanded to improve the stability of fluorescent nanosensors in aqueous solution. Practical application is the ultimate goal of developing nanosensors. Stable nanosensors can provide accurate detection results, which is promising for practical applications. Third, the toxicity of these fluorescent nanosensors to consumer health cannot be ignored, and requires considerable studies. Thus, low-toxic or non-toxic fluorescent nanomaterials are highly desirable. Forth, ratiometric fluorescence nanosensors are still highly desirable due to their accuracy. Most of the present nanosensors are fluorescence “turn-off”. The detection results of these nanosensors are easily disturbed by the fluctuation of the detection environment. Ratiometric fluorescence nanosensors can rule out the disturbance, producing more accurate results. Last but not least, many efforts should be devoted to developing portable nanosensors for point-of-care testing of BR, which is of great significance for persons in remote and developing areas. It is anticipated that the progress in detection systems could facilitate the rapid development of robust, commercially available, and sensitive BR assay kits in the future.

Yongming Guo: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing-original draft, Writing-review & editing. Chengwei Wei: Writing-original draft. Ruiqing Wang: Writing-original draft. Yubin Xiang: Funding acquisition, Writing-review & editing.
